msDesc.head: Six volumes of calligraphy collected by Colonel George William Hamilton (1807–1868).

msContents.summary:
These six volumes contain 107 calligraphy specimens written in Persian, Arabic, and Hindustani, collected by Colonel George William Hamilton (1807–1868) during his tenure in who served in India from 1823 to 1867. Ultimately appointed Commissioner of Delhi in 1862, he acquired nearly 1,200 oriental manuscripts, from which the British Museum purchased 352 from his widow, Charlotte Logie Hamilton (1817–1893), now mainly held in the British Library. These calligraphy specimens encompass both works by famed masters, and items presented to him as gifts by his subordinates and acquaintances. Several composition feature his name and titles, or praise him on certain occasions.
Alexander Lindsay, 25th Earl of Crawford and 8th earl of Balcarres (1812–1880), then purchased the remainder in 1868 for Bibliotheca Lindesiana at Haigh Hall, Wigan. In 1895, John Phillip Edmond (1850–1906), appointed librarian by Alexander's son and successor James Ludovic Lindsay, 26th Earl of Crawford and 9th Earl of Balcarres (1847–1913) selected and arranged the specimens and sent them to Henry Tennyson Folkard (1850-1916) , Librarian of the Wigan Free Public Library. Folkard replied how he commissioned Fazakerley of Liverpool, then owned and operated by John Fazakerley (1843–1909), to mount, bevel mat, and bind them in six royal folios volumes, opening hand-calligraphed title pages announcing the set as a gift from 'his Lordship', in which they still survive today.

msItem.title: Specimens of Oriental Penmanship
Specimens of Oriental Penmanship Arranged by the Right Hon. The Earl of Crawford and Presented by His Lordship to the Wigan Free Public Library

editor.persName: Lindsay, James Ludovic, 26th Earl of Crawford and 9th Earl of Balcarres, 1847–1913

supportDesc.support: Textblock comprised of heavy-weight machine-made, calendered paper mat boards manufactured in the Britain.
supportDesc.foliation: Some specimens numbered at top, in modern pencilled Arabic numerals, likely written by James Ludovic Lindsay, 26th Earl of Crawford (1847–1913) to arrange them, and followed for this record.
supportDesc.condition: Handle folios with caution. Fully interleaved. In fair but stable condition, with extensive acid migration stains and grime.

handDesc.handNote: Title pages written in in a bold black and red gothic hand.

binding.p: Mounted, bevel-matted, and bound in six uniform royal folio volumes by Fazakerley of Liverpool, then owned and operated by John Fazakerley (1843–1909), for Henry Tennyson Folkard (1850–1916), Librarian, Wigan Free Public Library.

Predominantly alizarin 'British'-patterned marbled endleaves, overprinted gilt and black veins added to the beginnings and ends, with all folios aattached to custom-made compensation guards, then oversewn on six recessed cords, laced into pasteboards. Edges ploughed then heads edge-gilt, with single-core decorative endbands in red, blue, and white threads over split cane cores at head and tail. Half bound in claret coloured, full naturally grained, goatskin leather, hollow-backed, with six false bands, and pebble-grained marroon starch-filled bookcloth sides.
binding.decoNote:
Spine panels paletted in thick blind and thin single fillets by the bands, with double fillets on the bands, and titled:
SPECIMENS
OF
ORIENTAL
PENMANSHIP.
Boards tooled with single blind and gold fillets by the cloth. Signed by the firm FAZAKERLEY BINDER LIVERPOOL in gold on the left board interiors, lower-right dentelles.

origin.p: Originally collected by Colonel George William Hamilton (1807-1868) who served in India from 1823 to 1867. Ultimately appointed Commissioner of Delhi in 1862, he acquired nearly 1,200 oriental manuscripts, from which the British Museum purchased 352 from his widow, Charlotte Logie Hamilton (1817–1893), now mostly held in the British Library.

provenance.p: Alexander Lindsay, 25th Earl of Crawford (1812–1880), purchased the remainder in 1868 for Bibliotheca Lindesiana at Haigh Hall, Wigan.

Inherited by James Ludovic Lindsay, 26th Earl of Crawford (1847–1913) who personally selected, arranged, and hired Fazakerley of Liverpool to bind the set. He then presented them as a gift that same year to the Wigan Free Public Library in 1895.

acquisition.p: Transferred to the Archives: Wigan & Leigh in 2023.

source.p: Bibliographical description by Jake Benson in 2025 with reference to the volumes in hand.

availability.p: To book an in-person or online appointment to consult the manuscript, visit . For any other enquiries please email archives@wigan.gov.uk

custEvent.p: Volumes III and VI exhibited in The Nature of Gothic: : Reflecting the Natural World in Historic and Contemporary Artistic Practice at the Blackburn Museum & Art Gallery, Cambridge in 2025.

listBibl.bibl: Jake Benson, 'Specimens of Oriental Penmanship in the Borough of Wigan: The Calligraphy Collection of Colonel George William Hamilton (1807–1868)' Persian Manuscripts in Britain (forthcoming, 2026).

Jake Benson and Christopher Bahl, 'Colonel George William Hamilton (1807–1868): Collecting Manuscripts in Nineteenth-Century South Asia.'
(forthcoming, 2026).

msPart.head: Volume I contains eighteen calligraphy specimens, Nos. 1-18.

binding.condition: Handle binding with caution. In good, stable condition, with slight abrasion to the exteriors and board edges and corners bumped.

msContents.summary: This unsigned fragment of ornate Persian prose written in shikastah-ta‘līq (broken-suspended) script, contains ten lines praising upright knowledge and actions.

msItem.title: Ornate Persian prose fragment
msItem.colophon: بتاریخ بیست و یکم ۲۱ شهر شوال سنه ۱۱۲۷ هجری

supportDesc.support: Written on dark tan-coloured, externally lightly sized and polished tinted paper.
supportDesc.condition: In poor condition with extensive breaks and losses.

layoutDesc.layout: Landscape format, written in 1 column in 10 lines. Ruled with a misṭarah hand guide.

history.origin: Probably completed in the Mughal Empire 21 Shawwāl 1127 AH (20 October 1715 CE).

msContents.summary: This ornate Persian prose composition quotes a passage of the Ḥabīb al-Siyar (Beloved of the Times) by Khvānd Mīr (ca. 1475–1535) concerning early Islamic philosopher Fārābī (ca. 870–950) known as 'Alpharabius' in Latin, followed by another paraphrased excerpt from the Ā'īn-i Akbar (Administrations of Akbar) by Abū al-Faz̤l (1551–1602), written in South Asian style shikastah-ta‘līq (broken-suspended) script by Ḥusayn for Rājah Mahānrā bin Sīrlām in Shāhjahānābād (Dehli) on 16 Ẕi al-Qa‘dah, year 26 of the reign of Shāh ‘Ālam II (1199 AH/20 September 1785 CE).

msItem.author:
Khvānd Mīr, Ghiyās̲ al-Dīn ibn Humām al-Dīn, ca. 1475–1535;
غیاث الدین ابن همام الدین میر خواند
msItem.title: Paraphrased passage from the Ḥabīb al-Siyar
msItem.author: Abū al-Faz̤l ibn Mubārak, 1551-1602
msItem.title: Paraphrased passage on calligraphy from the Ā'īn-i Akbarī
حبیب السیر
msItem.incipit: الله اکبر. رد حبیب سیر مخبر است که فارابی از مشاهر حکما اسلام است نام پرور نصر محمد ترخان یافت معلم هی فاراب بزعم بعضی از ارباب الباب نام شهریست بلاساغون و افول(؟) بعضی ولایتست در دیار ترکستان و مهارت در فنون حکمت و کمالات اولیه(؟) بود که عقل عقلین کامل کیاست مافوق آن درجه تصور نمیتوان گردد. در بعضی از تواریخ حبیب گشته در اواخر لام حیات بولایات شام رفت و در مجلس سیف الدوله لحظه برای بالاستاد در مباحثه دانشمندان نخل کرد سیف الدوله فرمود که بنشین گفت «کجا نشستم؟» سبف الدوله فرمود که «هر کجا فایل جلوس توان» ابو نصر در پهلوی سیف الدوله بر مسند سلطنت نشست و عرق و غیرت و نخوت پادشاهانه در حرکت آمده برای که متخصص آنها بود غلامان خود که این ترک ادب کرد چون از مجلس بیرون رود بسیاست رساند ابو نصر گفت «ايها الامیر اصبر فان الامور بعواقبها» سیف الدوله متعجب نمود و گفت «تو چه دانستی که من چه گفتم؟» گفت که «ما بجمیع من ز آنها و افقم(؟)‌ بعد از آن بعلما آغاز مناظره نمود فایق آمده و هرچه علماء ازو نصر شنیدند در سلک تحریر در آورده در وقت حاجت حجت باشد بعد از آ مقدمات سیف الدوله او را در سلک مخوصوصا آورده ابو نصر را نگاهداشته او را داخل ارباب و اهل سازوآواز را طلبیده فرمود سرود لسان نمود ابو نصر اعتراضات بر آن جماعت فرموده سیف الدوله پرسید که بر این علم نرسما است وقوفی هست از میان خود چند قطعه از آلات غنا بر آورده بر یکدیگر فصل نموده نواخت چندانکه اهل مجلس بخنده در آمده بعد از آن همان ز آلات بهیأت دیگر اتصال نموده نواخت که همی بجواب در افتاده سیف الدوله را واهی صحبت دلبین او خوش انده فرموودهرچه خواهی از من بطلب ولیکن ماهی در ملک من افتادید شما گفت ابو نصر بشرطی که مرا تکلیف ملازمت نکنی بر آنگاه دلبین حزب هر بر روشن است ...

این غنچه افرین که آنرا خط یافته چون عالم قدیم بی شروین است نه آورده مراتب است و نه آورده مهتاب در هر زمانی طایفه نوشتند و تهی و خاص در نمونش این حروف سر خاصه این چون خط هندی و یونانی و سریانی و قبطی و معقلی و عبری و کشمیری و کوفی و حبشی و ریحان(؟) و روحانی و غیر آن و در بعضی کتبت بالخیر برای آدم نسبت کتبت قضا بود بادریس که خط معقلی ترتیب داده به وسیله نماد که بیشتر تفاوت خط کوفی یک دانگ دور است باقی سطح است و معقلی تمام سطح و کتابهای عمارت کهنه به این خط است ...
msItem.colophon: در شاهجهانآباد تخویل راجه مهانرا بن سیرلام المری حسین مشق نمود بتاریخ بیست و یکم ۲۱ شهر شوال سنه ۲۶ شاه عالم.
Drafted in Shāhjahānābād (Dehli) for Rājah Mahānrā bin Sīrlām by al-Marrī Ḥusayn on 26 year Shāh ‘Ālam [II] (16 Ẕi al-Qa‘dah AH/20 September 1785 CE).

supportDesc.support: Written on externally sized and polished, finely sprayed red decorated paper.

layoutDesc.layout: Landscape format, written in 2 columns, the top with 21 oblique lines, the bottom nine horizontal. Ruled with a misṭarah hand guide.

handDesc.handNote: Written in shikastah ta‘līq script by al-Marrī Ḥusayn.

history.origin: Completed by al-Marrī Ḥusayn in Shāhjahānābād (Dehli) 21 Shawwāl 1127 AH (20 October 1715 CE).

msContents.summary: This unsigned densely written South Asian style shikastah-ta‘līq script relates an anecdote concerning Imām Mūsá al-Kāẓim ibn Jaʻfar (ca. 745–799).

msItem.title: Persian prose anecdote regarding Imām Muḥammad Musà Kāẓim
msItem.incipit: اورده‌اند که در زمانی که حضرت امام محمد موسی کاظم از دست امت را [؟] اختیار فرمود گزارش تعرف کوهستان(؟) اقبال ملاخظه فرمود که جمیعی از قوم نصاری سحایب(؟) گهروند خضرت پرسیدند که کجا میروید گویند بر بلای کوه را همی است

supportDesc.support: Written on ivory paper decorated with black and red spatters applied in tiny clusters, possibly by the calligrapher.
supportDesc.condition: In fair but stable condition with slight moisture damage, staining, and smearing.

layoutDesc.layout: Portrait format, written in 1 column in 14 lines. Ruled with a misṭarah hand guide.

handDesc.handNote: Written in hasty black shikastah ta‘līq script.

msContents.summary: This striking naskh script compisition in the style after Persian master Aḥmad Nayrīzī, paraphrases a popular quote attributed to medieval poet and satirist ‘Ubayd Zakānī (d. ca. 1370), regards five keys to happiness in life, reportedly displayed on a plaque by the pre-Islamic ruler Ānūshīrvān Khusraw (Khosrow I, King of Persia, ca. 484–579). An scribe named Muḥammad Walī Khān copied it in white lead on medium indigo blue paper in 1195 AH (1781 CE).

msItem.title: Saying attributed to ‘Ubayd Zākānī
msItem.colophon: Signed Muḥammad Walī Khān 1195 AH (1781 CE).
msItem.note: While several men of this name appear in historical sources, none comport with this date, hence he remains unidentified.

supportDesc.condition: In fair but stable condition with upper damaged areas overpainted.

layoutDesc.layout: Portrait format, written in 1 column, with additional lines written vertically at right and left at bottom. Ruled with a misṭarah hand guide.

handDesc.handNote: Written in bold white naskh script.

history.origin: Completed by Muḥammad Walī Khān, in 1195 AH (1781 CE)

msContents.summary: Arabic ḥadīth (Prophetic tradition) concerning the spiritual rewards for good handwriting written in bold thuluth script at top, which continues in smaller naskh in the centre, also bears Persian poetry obliquely written in shikastah on the right and left at bottom.

msItem.colophon: حرره المذنب الفقير المحتاج الى الله العلي خيدر قلى غفر له و لوالدينه
Undated but signed by Ḥaydar Qulī.
msItem.note: Probably not the same as Safavid scribe named Muḥammad ‘Ali bin Ḥaydar Qulī, who completed Bodleian Library MS. Elliott 63 in Shaʻbān 985 AH (October-November 1577 CE).

supportDesc.support: Finely silver-flecked paper, now tarnished. Mounted on a narrow dark indigo blue paper.
supportDesc.condition: In fair but stable condition with water damage along the bottom edge, and modest smudging.

layoutDesc.layout: Landscape format, written in 1 column at top, with 3 underneath, the top and bottom–middle horiztonal, with left and right bottom oblique. Ruled with a misṭarah hand guide.

handDesc.handNote: Written in bold and medium black thuluth and small shikastah scripts by Ḥaydar Qulī.

history.origin: Completed by Ḥaydar Qulī, location unidentified, but possibly Mughal Empire undated, but possibly 18th century.

msContents.summary: Arabic ḥadīth (Prophetic tradition) with Persian poetry. Signed at bottom by Muḥammad ‘Askarī in the third column from the right.

msItem.title: Arabic ḥadīth with Persian poetry
msItem.note: He studied under Muḥammad ‘Aṝif Yāqūt Raqam Khān according to Us̱mānī, p. 186, Nº 416.

supportDesc.condition: In fair but stable condition with extensive insect damage at left and smearing at bottom.

layoutDesc.layout: Landscape format, written in 1 column at top, with 5 underneath, the top and bottom–middle horiztonal, with the others oblique oblique. Ruled with a misṭarah hand guide.

handDesc.handNote: Written in bold and medium black thuluth and small shikastah scripts by Muḥammad ‘Askarī.

history.origin: Completed by Muḥammad ‘Askarī probably in Shāhjahānābād (Dehli) 21 Shawwāl 1127 AH (20 October 1715 CE) undated, but probably mid-19th century (ca. 1850–1867).

msContents.summary: Excerpt of a Persian congratulatory quatrain composed by Mīr ‘Alī Shīr Navā’ī (1441–1501) here copied in South Asian thuluth script by Muḥammad Faz̤l Ḥasan

msItem.title: Persian panegyric quatrain
msItem.incipit: هو الاکبر | جهان بكام و فلك بنده و ملك داعي * امید تازه و دولت قرین و بخت جوان | فتوح سوى يمين و سعود سوي يسار * شهر پیش رکاب و زمانه زیر عذان
msItem.note: Possibly the same scribe as noted by Us̱mānī, p. 188 Nº 426.

supportDesc.condition: In fair but stable condition with extensive insect damage at left and smearing at bottom.

layoutDesc.layout: Portrait format, written in 1 column with the colophon in 6 lines two final oblique lines at bottom. Ruled with a misṭarah hand guide.

handDesc.handNote: Written in bold South Asian black thuluth by Muḥammad Faz̤l Ḥasan.

history.origin: Completed by Muḥammad Faz̤l Ḥasan probably in Shāhjahānābād (Dehli), undated, but probably mid-19th century (ca. 1850–1867).

msContents.summary: This clever calligraphic rendering by an unidentified artist named ‘Abd al-Nabī ibn Ayyūb, depicts the epitaph of the tomb of poet Amīr Khusraw Dihlavī (ca. 1253-1325) with a poem by Shihāb al-Dīn bin Niẓām al-Dīn Haravī (d. ca. 1508–1509) concluding with a chronogram that equals 725 AH (1325 CE), the year that Amīr Khusraw passed away. Another panegyric poem composed by the tomb's architect, Khwajah Ṭāhir ‘Imād al-Dīn Ḥasan bin Sulṭān ‘Alī Sabzavarī, celebrates completion of the building in 1014 AH (1606 CE) appears in the surrounding margins, with the signature of the scribe at top-left.

msItem.title: Calligraphic Rendering of Amīr Khusraw Dihlavi's Epitaph

author.persName: Haravī, Shihāb al-Dīn bin Niẓām al-Dīn , d. 1508–1509
شهاب الدین بن نظام الدین هروی
Khwajah Ṭāhir ‘Imād al-Dīn Ḥasan bin Sulṭān ‘Alī Sabzavarī

msItem.note: Haravī's poem also appears in the conclusion of Rylands Persian MS 109, appended to a copy of the Dīvān of Amīr Khusraw's poems.

supportDesc.support: Gently polished ivoury coloured wove paper, hand-tinted with watercolours.

layoutDesc.layout: Portrait format, written in 1 column within the epitaph and with surrounding lines in the margins.

handDesc.handNote: Written in fine nasta‘līq script by ‘Abd al-Nabī ibn Ayyūb.

decoDesc.decoNote: This composition employs watercolour washes to lend greater realism.

history.origin: Completed by ‘Abd al-Nabī ibn Ayyūb in Shāhjahānābād (Dehli), undated, but probably mid-19th century (ca. 1850–1867).

msContents.summary: Persian prose didactic aphorism concerns child-rearing, copied by Rā'i Rājā Lāl Jī (1838–1885), son of Jīvanalāla Munshī Rā'ī Bahādur (1806–1884), who served as secretary to the last Mughal ruler and as a British informant who reported on events as they unfolded in the Red Fort in Delhi during the rebellion of 1857.

msItem.title: Persian didactic prose
msItem.incipit: پنج خصلت که در كودكان اند اگر در بزرگان بودی اولياء الله بودی...
msItem.colophon: کتبه المذنب راجا لال عفی عنه

supportDesc.support: Partly polished pink laid paper, with 16 laid line per 20 mm. Unruled.

layoutDesc.layout: Portrait format, written in 1 column in six lines.

handDesc.handNote: Written in medium-sized black nasta‘līq script by Rā'i Rājā Lāl Jī.

decoDesc.decoNote: Patches of irregularly cut squared of gold leaf embellish the corners, under the basmala, and between the lines, the last outlined in black, reverse-scalloped strokes designed to recall cloud bands.

history.origin: Completed by Rā'i Rājā Lāl Jī probably in Shāhjahānābād (Dehli), undated, but probably mid-19th century (ca. 1850–1867).

msContents.summary: An excerpt from the Ḥadīqat al-Aqālīm (Enclosed Garden of the Climes) by Murtaz̤ā Ilahyār Ḥusayn Bilgrāmī (ca. 1720–1790) concerns five good works that foster courtesy and respect, copied by Rā'i Rājā Lāl Jī (1838–1885), son of Jīvanalāla Munshī Rā'ī Bahādur (1806–1884).

msItem.title: Excerpt from the Ḥadīqat al-Aqālīm (Enclosed Garden of the Climes)
msItem.author:
Bilgrāmī, Murtaz̤ā Ilahyār Ḥusayn, ca. 1720–1790;
مرتضى حسين الآهیار بلگرامی
msItem.incipit: این چند چیز‌ها بهترین اعمال است روندادن به مردم ب ...
msItem.colophon: ذره بمقدار راجا لال پسر منشی جیون لعل
msItem.note: The scribe explicitly names his father here in the colophon: Rājā Lāl pisar-i Munshī Jiwān La‘l (Rājā Lāl son of Munshī Jiwān La‘l).

supportDesc.support: Very fine, thin handmade paper, ~32 laid lines per 20 mm. Unruled.

layoutDesc.layout: Portrait format, written in 1 column with 9 lines.

handDesc.handNote: Written in medium-sized black nasta‘līq script by Rā'i Rājā Lāl Jī.

decoDesc.decoNote: Each line is ruled horizontally in gold outlined in thin black, while foliate decoration in blue appears by the colophon at bottom.

history.origin: Completed by Rā'i Rājā Lāl Jī probably in Shāhjahānābād (Dehli), undated, but probably mid-19th century (ca. 1850–1867).

msContents.summary: Hindustani couplet in praise of Persian, Turkish, and Sindhi languages ascribed to Amīr Khusraw Dihlavī (ca. 1253–1325) concerns five good works that foster courtesy and respect, copied by Rā'i Rājā Lāl Jī (1838–1885), son of Jīvanalāla Munshī Rā'ī Bahādur (1806–1884).

msItem.title: Excerpt from the Ḥadīqat al-Aqālīm (Enclosed Garden of the Climes)
msItem.author:
Amīr Khusraw Dihlavī, ca. 1253–1325;
امیر خسرو دهلوی
msItem.incipit: الله اکبر عز اسمه | پهیلی آینه من تصانیف حضرت امیر خسرو رخمت الله علیه: «فارسی اوسکی آی نانہہ۔۔۔».
msItem.colophon: حرره فدوی راجا لال سر‌رشته دار مال ضلعدہلی لعل
msItem.note: This composition indicates he was appointed Treasurer of Delhi by the British Raj government, hence probably written when a direct subordinate to Hamilton.

supportDesc.support: Very fine, thin, bright viridian green cross-grained laid paper, Cross-grain laid paper, ~24 laid lines per 20 mm and 23 mm between chain lines..

layoutDesc.layout: Portrait format, written in 1 column with 7 lines.

handDesc.handNote: Written in small and medium-sized black nasta‘līq script by Rā'i Rājā Lāl Jī.

history.origin: Completed by Rā'i Rājā Lāl Jī probably in Shāhjahānābād (Dehli), undated, but probably mid-19th century (ca. 1850–1867).

msContents.summary: The former Mughal Chief Justice Muḥammad Ṣadr al-Dīn Khān Āzurdah (1789–1868) composed this ornate Arabic prose panegyric composition in honour of Colonel George William Hamilton (1807–1868) written on paper embellished with gold squares and silver flecks, on 16 Nov. 1862.

msItem.title: Arabic panegyric prose praising Col. George William Hamilton
msItem.author:
Muḥammad Ṣadr al-Dīn Khān Āzurdah, 1789–1868;
محمد صدر الدین آزرده
msItem.note: This composition probably pertains to the period when the author successfully appealed for the restoration of his properties seized by the government after 1857, with the notable exception of his library, which now constitutes a primary portion of the Delhi Collection in the British Library, for which see Shapouran.

supportDesc.support: Very fine, thin, bright viridian green cross-grained laid paper, Cross-grain laid paper, ~24 laid lines per 20 mm and 23 mm between chain lines..
supportDesc.condition: In overall good condition, albeit with areas where adhesive struck through the paper fibres when mounted.

layoutDesc.layout: Portrait format, written in 1 column with 12 lines.

handDesc.handNote: Written in small and medium-sized black nasta‘līq script by Muḥammad Ṣadr al-Dīn Khān Āzurdah.

decoDesc.decoNote: Written on domestically manufactured sized and polished letter-paper embellished with four rows of four positioned cut gold leaf diamonds and flecks of silver.

history.origin: Completed by Muḥammad Ṣadr al-Dīn Khān Āzurdah probably in Shāhjahānābād (Dehli), 16 Nov. 1862.

msContents.summary: Unsigned Persian panegyric couplet, possibly by Azurdah (Nº 12), Baldeo Singh (Nº 15 and 17), or Kishan Prashad (Nº 16), given the hand and choice of paper.

msItem.title: Persian panegyric couplet
msItem.incipit: اقبال تو پاینده و تو جوان باد * حکمت چو قضا بر همه احکام روا باد

supportDesc.support: Very evenly formed straight-grained domestically manufactured sized and highly polished letter-paper.

layoutDesc.layout: Portrait format, written in 1 column with 2 lines.

handDesc.handNote: Written in small black nasta‘līq script.

decoDesc.decoNote: Written on domestically manufactured sized and polished letter-paper embellished with three rows of four equidistantally positioned cut gold leaf diamonds with irregular flecks of silver leaf between.

history.origin: Probably completed in Shāhjahānābād (Dehli), undated, but probably mid-19th century (ca. 1850–1867).

msContents.summary: Unsigned Persian panegyric couplet of Muḥammad ʻAlī Ḥazīn Lāhījī (ca. 1692–1766), possibly copied by Azurdah (Nº 12), Baldeo Singh (Nº 15 and 17), or Kishan Prashad (Nº 16), given the hand and choice of paper.

msItem.title: Persian panegyric
msItem.incipit: عمرت دراز باد که تا دور مشتری * ما از تو بر خوریم تو از عمر بر خوریم

supportDesc.support: Very evenly formed straight-grained domestically manufactured sized and highly polished letter-paper.

layoutDesc.layout: Portrait format, written in 1 column with 2 lines.

handDesc.handNote: Written in small black nasta‘līq script.

decoDesc.decoNote: Written on domestically manufactured sized and polished letter-paper embellished with three rows of four equidistantally positioned cut gold leaf diamonds with irregular flecks of gold leaf between.

history.origin: Probably completed in Shāhjahānābād (Dehli), undated, but probably mid-19th century (ca. 1850–1867).

msContents.summary: An original Persian panegyric quintain (mukhammis) praising the first day of Spring and the Persian New Year (Nawrūz), signed by Baldeo Singh, son of Delhi merchant Zurawar Singh, written in three columns, with Arabic colophon underneath.

msItem.title: Mukhammis
مخمس
Quintain
msItem.incipit: نوروز که روز دلفروز است * خوشتر ز شب وصال آمد
msItem.colophon: رقمه بلدیو سنگہ خلف زورآور سنگ ساهوی دهلی
msItem.note: The scribe explicitly names his father in the colophon: Baldīyū Singh khalaf-i Lalah Zūrāvar Singh Sāhū-yi Dihlī.

supportDesc.support: Very evenly formed straight-grained domestically manufactured sized and highly polished letter-paper.

layoutDesc.layout: Portrait format, rotated 90º counter-clockwise, written in 3 columns at top, two oblique at left and right contain four hemistichs each, with another couplet narrowly written vertically in the middle between, and the colophon underneath.

handDesc.handNote: Written in small black nasta‘līq script by Baldīyū Singh.

decoDesc.decoNote: Written on domestically manufactured sized and polished letter-paper embellished with three rows of four equidistantally positioned cut gold leaf diamonds with irregular flecks of silver leaf between.

history.origin: Completed by Baldeo Singh, probably in Shāhjahānābād (Dehli), undated, but probably mid-19th century (ca. 1850–1867).

msContents.summary: Panegyric Persian quatrain (rubā‘ī) by Khalīfah Shāh Muḥammad Kanawjī (fl. 17th century) excerpted from his Jāmiʻ al-Qawānīn copied by Kishan Prashad.

msItem.title: Jāmiʻ al-Qawānīn
جامع القوانين
Inshā-yi Khalīfah
انشای خلیفه
msItem.author:
Kanawjī, Khalīfah Shāh Muḥammad, fl. 17th century;
قنوجی، خلیفه شاه محمد
msItem.incipit: ای آمدنت باعث آبادی ما * ذکر تو بود زمزمنه شادی ما | ما را چه بود که در نثارت سازیم؟ * قربان سر تو باد آزادی ما
msItem.colophon: فدوی کشن پرشاد زهی عز و شرف گر قبول افتاد
msItem.note: While Us̱mānī p. 170 no. 329, reports a calligrapher of the same name, Rajah Kishan Prashad (1864–1940), it cannot be him but another man of the same name.

supportDesc.support: Very evenly formed straight-grained domestically manufactured sized and highly polished letter-paper.

layoutDesc.layout: Portrait format, rotated 90º counter-clockwise, written in 1 column in four oblique lines, with the colophon underneath.

handDesc.handNote: Written in small black nasta‘līq script by Kishan Prashād.

decoDesc.decoNote: Written on domestically manufactured sized and polished letter-paper embellished with three rows of four equidistantally positioned cut gold leaf diamonds with irregular flecks of silver leaf between.

history.origin: Completed by Kishan Prashād, probably in Shāhjahānābād (Dehli), undated, but probably mid-19th century (ca. 1850–1867).

msContents.summary: Baldeo Singh, son of Delhi merchant Zurawar Singh composed this original Persian panegyric ode (qaṣīdah) which while not explicitly naming him, the sixth and seventh couplets describe an appointment in Delhi as regent, hence it probably celebrates the appointment of Colonel George William Hamilton (1807–1868) as Commissioner of Delhi in 1862.

msItem.title: Qaṣīdah Praising George William Hamilton
msItem.incipit: سپیده دم که بخند و گل از هبوب نسیم ...
msItem.colophon: رقمه بلدیو سنگہ خلف زورآور سنگ ساهوی دهلی
msItem.note: The scribe explicitly names his father in the colophon: Baldīyū Singh khalaf-i Lalah Zūrāvar Singh Sāhū-yi Dihlī.

supportDesc.support: Very evenly formed straight-grained domestically manufactured sized and highly polished letter-paper.

layoutDesc.layout: Landscape format, rotated 90º counter-clockwise, written in 4 horiztonal columns, alternating with wide oblique lines with 18 hemistichs each, narrow horizontal lines, and colophon underneath.

handDesc.handNote: Written in small black nasta‘līq script by Baldīyū Singh.

decoDesc.decoNote: Embellished with four rows of four equidistantally positioned cut gold leaf diamonds with irregular flecks of silver leaf between.

history.origin: Completed by Baldeo Singh, in Shāhjahānābād (Dehli), undated, but probably 1862.

msContents.summary: This anonymous Persian lyric poem (ghazal) celebrates the appointment of Colonel George William Hamilton (1807–1868) as Commissioner of Delhi in 1862.

msItem.title: Ghazal Praising George William Hamilton
msItem.incipit: اقبال تو پاینده و تو جوان باد * حکمت چو قضا بر همه احکام روا باد
زهی جناب هملتین کمشنر ذی شان * که گرگ و میش ز عدلش شد است با هم رام

supportDesc.support: Written on European-made mottled blue, flocked laid paper with ~18 laid lines per 20 mm and 25 mm between chain lines.

layoutDesc.layout: Portrait format, rotated 90º counter-clockwise, written in 2 oblique columns with columns, alternating with wide oblique lines with 18 columns with 16 hemistichs each. Unruled.

history.origin: Probably completed in Shāhjahānābād (Dehli), undated, but probably 1862.

msPart.head: Volume II contains eighteen calligraphy specimens, Nos. 19-36.

binding.condition: Handle binding with condition. In fair but stable condition, with extensive staining, abrasion, and salts on the exterior, probably due to prolonged exposure to moisture.

msPart.head: Volume III contains eighteen calligraphy specimens, Nos. 37-54.

binding.condition: Handle binding with condition. In good, stable condition, with slight abrasion to the exteriors and board edges.

msPart.head: Volume IV contains eighteen calligraphy specimens, Nos. 55-72.

binding.condition: Handle binding with condition. In good, stable condition, with slight abrasion on the exteriors and board edges.

msPart.head: Volume V contains eighteen calligraphy specimens, Nos. 73-90.

binding.condition: Handle binding with condition. In good, stable condition, with slight abrasion to the exteriors and board edges.

msPart.head: Volume VI, the final one of the set, contains seventeen calligraphy specimens, Nos. 91-107.

binding.condition: Handle binding with condition. In good, stable condition, with slight abrasion to the exteriors and board edges.
